name: Cordova
short_description: Rockhound the Mississippi River bluffs near Cordova for Ordovician chert, jasper, and quartz specimens. Easy access to Illinois Upper Peninsula fossil-bearing terrain.
description:
Cordova sits in the heart of Rock Island County along the Mississippi River valley, where Ordovician and Silurian age bedrock creates abundant opportunities for rockhounders. The area around Cordova offers access to bluff exposures and tributary washes rich in chert, jasper, and fossil-bearing material from shallow marine sediments deposited 450+ million years ago.

The Mississippi River valley here is characterized by low, rolling terrain at approximately 585 feet elevation with flat topography ideal for beginners. Public land access along the river corridor and state conservation areas provides rockhounding opportunities without requiring permits or fees. Collectors should focus on eroded bluffs, stream beds, and loose talus slopes where weathering has freed colorful specimens.

This location is particularly rewarding for those seeking quality chert in reds, grays, and browns, along with occasional jasper nodules and petrified wood fragments. The flat terrain and proximity to parking areas make Cordova an excellent choice for families and younger rockhounders building their collections. Spring rains and seasonal flooding expose fresh material along riverside deposits throughout the year.

The bedrock geology reflects shallow Paleozoic seas that once covered Illinois, leaving behind silica-rich sediments and nodules. Unlike harder western rockhounding sites, Cordova rewards patient surface hunting over challenging extraction work.
history: The Mississippi River valley near Cordova exposes Ordovician limestone and chert deposits formed approximately 450-470 million years ago during the Ordovician transgression, when shallow seas covered the North American craton. Quaternary glaciation and post-glacial river erosion have exposed these Paleozoic bedrock formations and created the bluff system that makes surface collecting possible today.
tips: Search the base of exposed bluffs and along streambank deposits where weathered chert and jasper naturally collect in loose gravels - these areas require no digging. Use a 5-pound hand hammer or rock pick to test loose nodules for quality before attempting extraction; many specimens shatter or contain poor color beneath weathered rinds. Look for angular chert fragments showing translucent reds, honey tones, and banded grays in creekbeds and talus piles. Bring a magnet to distinguish magnetic iron oxide specimens from lower-quality material, and focus morning searches before afternoon sun makes dark-colored rocks harder to spot.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently rolling terrain at 585 feet elevation with Mississippi River bottomland topography. Walking distances from parking areas to productive bluffs typically under 0.5 miles. Footing is easy on flat ground but uneven and potentially muddy near creeks and eroded slopes after rain. Loose rock and crumbling shale require careful footwork on bluff faces. Vegetation includes river corridor trees and understory typical of Illinois floodplain zones.
best_season: March - May and September - November. Spring provides fresh exposed material after winter freeze-thaw and spring rains; creeks and washes carry eroded specimens. Fall offers comfortable temperatures and lower humidity than summer. Summer heat and humidity make midday hunting uncomfortable, while winter ice and frozen ground limit access to some areas.
written_directions: From Rock Island, take Illinois Route 67 northwest toward Cordova for approximately 8 miles. Continue through Cordova village and look for river access points or public land parking areas near the Mississippi River on the east side of town. Follow local road signs toward boat landings or state conservation areas. Parking is typically available at informal pullouts near bluff exposures or along secondary roads providing river access. Do not block private farm gates or trespass on marked private land.
road_conditions: Paved state routes and secondary roads in good condition. Local access roads to river may be gravel or unpaved; standard passenger vehicles adequate for most access points.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along river access roads. Some areas offer designated boat launch parking areas with limited capacity. Park away from private property and farm gates. No developed parking facilities; expect 2-6 parking spaces at typical locations.
collection_rules: Public land rockhounding is permitted for personal use only. Take only what you can carry. Do not trespass on private land. Do not remove material from marked state conservation areas if collection is prohibited by posted signs. Respect all no-entry barriers and private property boundaries.
rules:
Personal use collection only
No commercial resale of specimens
Do not damage bedrock or create hazardous excavations
Respect private property - only collect on public or authorized land
Leave no trash
Check for posted collection restrictions at specific sites
nearest_services: Rock Island, Illinois, approximately 8 miles south. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water available. Cordova village has limited services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families with young children. No significant elevation gain. Unpaved or informal parking areas near river access. Bluff faces may be steep and unstable; do not climb actively eroding slopes. Stream crossing may be necessary after heavy rain. Muddy footing common near water. Sturdy hiking shoes recommended but not essential for level walking.

faq.question: What rockhounding gems can I find near Cordova Illinois along the Mississippi River?
faq.answer: Cordova is known for quality chert in reds, grays, and browns, along with jasper nodules and occasional petrified wood. Quartz specimens are also common. These specimens erode naturally from Ordovician-age bedrock exposed in river bluffs and wash deposits, making them easy to find without permits or fees on public land.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Cordova Illinois free or do I need to pay a fee?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Cordova is free and requires no permits. This is public land rockhounding along the Mississippi River where personal use collection is allowed. Simply park at river access areas and hunt the bluffs and creek beds - no commercial license or day fee required.

faq.question: Can I bring my family and kids rockhounding in Cordova Illinois?
faq.answer: Yes, Cordova is an excellent family rockhounding destination. The flat terrain, short walking distances from parking, and abundant loose specimens on the surface make it ideal for children ages 6 and up. No climbing or dangerous digging is necessary - most specimens can be collected by hand or with a simple rock hammer.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Cordova Illinois?
faq.answer: March through May and September through November are ideal. Spring rains and freeze-thaw cycles expose fresh material in bluffs and creek beds. Fall offers comfortable temperatures. Summer heat and humidity make afternoon collecting difficult, while winter ice and muddy conditions limit access to some river areas.

faq.question: What tools and gear do I need for rockhounding at Cordova?
faq.answer: Bring a 5-pound hand hammer or rock pick to test loose nodules, a durable carrying bag or backpack, sturdy hiking shoes (especially after rain when ground is muddy), work gloves, and a magnet to identify quality specimens. A small sifting screen helps separate quality chert from weathered material in creek gravels. Sunscreen and water are essential year-round.

faq.question: How far is Cordova Illinois from Rock Island and what kind of rockhounding geology should I expect?
faq.answer: Cordova is approximately 8 miles north of Rock Island on Illinois Route 67. The area sits in the Mississippi River valley with Ordovician bedrock 450+ million years old, exposing chert, jasper, and quartz formed in ancient shallow seas. Quaternary glaciation and river erosion have created accessible bluff exposures perfect for surface collecting without difficult extraction work.

meta_title: Cordova Illinois Rockhounding - Free Chert & Jasper
meta_description: Hunt Ordovician chert, jasper, and quartz at Cordova Illinois. Free public land rockhounding on Mississippi River bluffs. Perfect for families and beginners.
